coredata.dc:title: Biosensing of solitary and clustered abasic site DNA damage lesions with copper nanoclusters and carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, Seema
Singh, Manoj K.
Das, Prolay
coredata.dc:description: Accumulation of potentially mutagenic abasic sites lesions in DNA are often considered as stress induced biomarker for radiation exposure and carcinogenesis. A simple and cost-effective fluorescence based method for detection of abasic sites in dsDNA is demonstrated. DNA was used as a template for copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) formation. Presence of abasic sites in DNA hinder the formation of Cu NCs resulting in increased presence of Cu(II) in solution. These free copper ions were traced quantitatively by fluorescence quenching of carbon dots (CDs) in solution that report the presence of abasic sites in those DNA samples. Apart from fluorescence properties, binding of Cu NCs are markedly different for normal and abasic sites containing DNA. To demonstrate the inclusiveness of Cu NCs and CD based biosensing of abasic sites, oligomeric DNA, plasmid DNA in linear and condensed form and DNA extracted from onion and HeLa cells were tested and respond sensitively to the presence of abasic sites in nanomolar range of those DNA that is visually noticeable with UV light. The detection strategy works very well for clustered abasic sites DNA damage also, where multiple abasic sites lesions are present in close proximity in DNA and easily detectable by this method through visual inspection under UV light.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Abasic site
Copper nanoclusters
Carbon dots
pUC19
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Introduction
Materials and methods
Material and reagents
Experimental measurements
ITC measurements
Creation and analysis of abasic site in oligonucleotides
Extraction of DNA from onion and purification
Extraction of DNA from HeLa cells
Creation of abasic site in pUC19, plant (onion) DNA and DNA extracted from HeLa cells
DNA condensate formation from linear pUC19
Hydrothermal synthesis of N,S co-doped CDs
Formation of Cu NCs and estimation by CDs
Results and discussion
Creation and analysis of abasic sites in DNA
Detection of DNA abasic sites by Cu NCs and CDs
Synthesis and characterisation of Cu NCs in oligonucleotide duplex
Fluorescence quenching of CDs in presence of abasic sites in DNA
Change in conformational and duplex stability of DNA induced by Cu NCs formation
Binding efficiency of Cu NCs to DNA
Estimation of abasic sites in plasmid, plant genomic DNA and DNA from HeLa cells
Fluorescence quenching of CDs in presence of abasic sites in plasmid and plant genomic DNA
Fluorescence quenching of CDs in presence of abasic sites in real biological sample
Conclusion
Author contributions
Acknowledgments
Supplementary data
References

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CDs aided estimation of Cu NCs formation on DNA template with and without clustered Abasic sites.

Proof of abasic site generation in various forms of DNA A. 20% PAGE showing abasic site cleavage in C1ab and C1abC2ab by APE1 enzyme B. 1.5% agarose gel showing fragmentation of abasic site containing linearized pUC19 after NaOH treatment and C. 1.5% agarose gel for onion genomic DNA after abasic site generation and NaOH treatment.

A. UV–vis B. Fluorescence spectra (λex=340nm) of Cu NCs formed on DNA templates C.&D. TEM image of Cu NCs formed on C1C2and C1abC2ab DNA templates respectively and E. XPS spectra of Cu NCs on C1C2 and C1abC2ab DNA templates.

A. Quenching of fluorescence intensity of CDs (λex=330nm) due to free copper ions in Cu NCs reaction mixture B. Plot of % decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs in presence of abasic sites in DNA C. Visual comparison showing fluorescence of Cu NC in C1C2, C1ab-C2, C1-C2ab and C1ab-C2ab from left to right & D. Corresponding fluorescence spectra of the Cu NCs samples E. Visual comparison showing fluorescence of same solution after addition of CD indicating fluorescence quenching of CDs in Cu NCs samples and F. Corresponding fluorescence spectra of the CDs.
Circular dichroism spectra showing conformational changes in dsDNA on nanoclusters formation A. C1C2 B. C1abC2 C. C1C2ab and D. C1abC2ab.
First derivative of thermal melting graph showing change in thermal melting temperature of dsDNA before and after Cu NCs formation on a. C1C2 b. C1abC2 c. C1C2ab and d. C1abC2ab.
Isothermal titration calorimetry graph for Cu NCs formation in A. C1-C2 and B. C1ab-C2ab DNA templates.

A. Graph showing decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs on interaction with Cu NCs on plasmid and plant DNA B. Plot of % decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs with Cu NCs on different DNA templates and visual comparison of fluorescence quenching of CDs in Cu NC templated on C. pUC19 and abpUC19 D. onion DNA and abasic onion DNA.

A. Spectra showing decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs on interaction with Cu NCs on HDNA (DNA extracted from HeLa Cells) and abHDNA (abasic sites containing DNA from HeLa cells) and B. corresponding visual comparison of fluorescence quenching of CDs.
Oligomeric DNA sequences used for template formation.

row.entry: 5′- AGC TGT ACG AGT GCA CGA TAC GAT CCA GAT ACT CAG CAT -3′
5′- ATG CTG AGT ATC TGG ATC GTA TCG TGC ACT CGT AC -3′
5′- AGC TGT ACG AGU GCA CGA TAC GAT CCA GAT ACT CAG CAT -3′
5′- ATG CTG AGT ATC TGG ATC GTA TCG UGC ACT CGT AC -3′

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of present study with other DNA abasic site sensing methods.

row.entry: Abasic sites were tagged with biotinylated aldehyde reactive probes and monomeric avidin followed by analysis using atomic force microscope.
The method exploits the reaction of abasic sites with [14C] methoxyamine to form stable oxime derivatives.
The abasic sites were induced in DNA and tagged with a fluorescent aldehyde-reactive probe (FARP).
Selective attachment of 2-aminomethyl-18-crown-6 (18c6) to abasic site.
Electrophoretic translocation through the bacterial protein ion channel α-hemolysin (α-HL) embedded in a lipid bilayer.
9-dicyanovinyljulolidine (DCVJ) selectively target the apurinic site and its fluorescence is enhanced when the flanking base is guanine.
Near infrared (NIR) fluorescent probe that binds to apurinic/apyrimidinic (AP) site.
Biocompatible and economical fluorescent probes Cu NCs and CDs were used to detect abasic sites in DNA.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Thermodynamic parameters for binding of Cu(II) to dsDNA template for Cu NCs formation for A. C1C2 and B. C1abC2ab.

head.ce:title: Biosensing of solitary and clustered abasic site DNA damage lesions with copper nanoclusters and carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, Patna 801103, Bihar, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Atomic and Molecular Physics Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Mumbai 400085, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accumulation of potentially mutagenic abasic sites lesions in DNA are often considered as stress induced biomarker for radiation exposure and carcinogenesis. A simple and cost-effective fluorescence based method for detection of abasic sites in dsDNA is demonstrated. DNA was used as a template for copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) formation. Presence of abasic sites in DNA hinder the formation of Cu NCs resulting in increased presence of Cu(II) in solution. These free copper ions were traced quantitatively by fluorescence quenching of carbon dots (CDs) in solution that report the presence of abasic sites in those DNA samples. Apart from fluorescence properties, binding of Cu NCs are markedly different for normal and abasic sites containing DNA. To demonstrate the inclusiveness of Cu NCs and CD based biosensing of abasic sites, oligomeric DNA, plasmid DNA in linear and condensed form and DNA extracted from onion and HeLa cells were tested and respond sensitively to the presence of abasic sites in nanomolar range of those DNA that is visually noticeable with UV light. The detection strategy works very well for clustered abasic sites DNA damage also, where multiple abasic sites lesions are present in close proximity in DNA and easily detectable by this method through visual inspection under UV light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Abasic sites, also known as apurinic/apyrimidinic sites are one of the most prevalent type of DNA damages where purine or pyrimidine bases of DNA are removed by cleavage of sugar-base glycosidic linkage from the DNA backbone. Abasic sites in DNA are created due to exposure to radiation, oxidative stress, chemical agents or even induced endogenously [1–3]. A single mammalian cell produces more than 104 abasic sites per day [4,5]. Clustered abasic sites − very closely spaced multiple abasic sites within few helical turns of DNA results mainly from ionizing radiations, and constitute complex repair resistant damage to DNA [6,7]. DNA containing numerous solitary or clustered abasic sites may potentially induce chromosomal abnormalities, mutations, aging related diseases and metastasis [8–12]. Thus, detecting abasic sites in DNA is crucial for diagnosis of many diseases including immunodeficiency, cancer and for precautions and preservation of genomic integrity [13]. Consequently, development of tools to detect abasic site in DNA are of broad interest to the scientific communities. Herein, we report an easy, cost effective and visually detectable method for biosensing of abasic site damaged lesion in DNA by using copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) and carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescent probes.

Several methods have been developed for the detection of abasic sites which include aldehyde specific reagent (ARP), terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase dUTP nick end labelling (TUNEL) assay, polymerase chain reaction (PCR), mass spectrometry, electrochemistry, comet assay, atomic force microscopy (AFM) etc [14–17]. The chemical features of the abasic site have been utilized as a target to develop most of the molecular probes to detect these lesions. Biotinylated ARP have been utilized as a key probe for detection of abasic sites by using AFM, capillary electrophoresis etc [15,18]. Several tags having high binding affinity with abasic sites in DNA have been used for sensitive detection of these lesions using various analytical techniques [19–21]. Recently, fluorescence based methods have been used that includes small organic molecules as ligands and inorganic fluorophores for selective sensing of abasic sites in DNA [22–26]. However, tedious process, costly equipment and sensitivity issues may limit their applications. This calls for simple and fast detection strategies for abasic sites and other types of DNA damage lesions. The method described here for biosensing of abasic site in DNA is a label free detection method that utilize the concept of fluorescence quenching of highly fluorescent CDs in presence of Cu(II) ions that are unable to be incorporated in Cu NC formation due to the presence of abasic sites in DNA. Following this method, for the first time the presence of abasic sites in DNA could be visually detected in presence of only UV light without any instruments.

CuNCs have emerged as a new tool in the recent expansion of nanotechnology with superior optical properties, water solubility and biocompatibility [27]. Cu NCs have shown potential fundamental applications in optics, electronics and biotechnology [28–30]. The nanoscale size, facile route of synthesis, and biocompatibility of Cu NCs are some of the fascinating properties that paved the way for Cu NCs being utilized for catalysis, detection of metal ions, biomolecular imaging and pH sensing [31,32]. Further, the abundance and low cost of the precursors are encouraging for broad scale applications of Cu NCs [33]. Similarly, CDs with excellent water solubility, low cost synthesis and biocompatibility, has shown immense promise as a nanoparticle from the carbon family for biomedical imaging and other applications [34]. Reportedly, CDs have been researched and utilized for drug delivery, gene delivery, metal sensing, polymer composites formation, DNA damage detection and hydrogel formation [35–38]. Notably, both CuNCs and CDs having high photostability, excellent fluorescence properties and low toxicity that are good replacements for toxic heavy metal based quantum dots and photodegradable dyes. Thus, the admirable properties of these two fluorophores were integrated to construct a smart biosensing platform for the detection of abasic sites in DNA.

The present study is a simple and fast detection strategy of solitary and clustered abasic sites in DNA by using Cu NCs and CDs (Scheme 1 ). The aqueous soluble Cu NCs were synthesized on multiform of DNA as a template. The templated formation of CuNCs was achieved on oligonucleotide (containing single or multiple abasic sites), linearized and condensed form of pUC19 plasmid and DNA extracted from onion cells (plant genomic DNA) and HeLa cells. While abasic sites in precise location in the oligonucleotide were created by using uridine DNA glycosylase enzyme (UDG treatment on deoxyuridine residues in the DNA strands), those in the other DNA samples were generated by chemical treatment. The presence of abasic sites extant a hindrance in the templated synthesis of Cu NCs that results in the presence of free unreacted Cu(II) ions in the solution. The free unreacted Cu(II) in the reaction mixture (not included in NCs formation) was found to be responsible for the quantitative quenching of CDs in solution. This quenching is visually detectable in the presence of UV light and indicates the presence of abasic sites in a DNA sample. Even, the presence of one or two abasic sites in an oligomer duplex DNA (∼35 base pair) is detectable that make the system decidedly sensitive. Moreover, for the first time we evaluated the thermodynamic parameters of the formation of Cu NCs on DNA template that clearly shows a sharp reduction in binding of Cu NCs to DNA having abasic sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Material and reagents
ce:section.ce:para: Citric acid monohydrate, cysteamine, copper (II) sulfate pentahydrate CuSO4·5H2O, 3-(N-morpholino) propane sulfonic acid (MOPS), ascorbic acid and spermidine were purchased from Sigma. pUC19 plasmid, uracil DNA glycosylase (UDG), HindIII restriction endonuclease and Human apurinic/apyrimidinic endonuclease (APE1) enzyme were purchased from New England Biolabs, USA. Nanopure water from Millipore was used in all experiments. The oligomeric DNA sequences was obtained from Sigma custom oligo service (Table 1 ). Abasic sites in C1 and C2 was generated by replacing the Thymine (T) by Uracil (U) bases in precise locations and subsequent UDG enzyme treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental measurements
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained on an UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Steady-state fluorescence spectra were measured on a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba, Japan). Samples for transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis of Cu NCs were prepared by drop-casting the solution of Cu NCs on carbon coated copper grids. TEM measurement was performed on Philips CM-200 at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The oxidation state of Cu in Cu NCs reaction mixture was analysed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Kratos Analytical, UK) with monochromatic Al Kα as an X-ray source (75W). The analysis was performed under high vacuum of 2×10−7 Pa. The induction of abasic sites in oligomer DNA was examined by 20% polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) in 1X tris taurine EDTA (TTE) buffer. Similarly, abasic site formation in pUC19 and onion DNA was analysed by 1.5% agarose gel electrophoresis in 1X tris acetate EDTA (TAE) buffer at 120V and images were accessed using a CCD camera fitted with a gel documentation system (UVP, UK). Hydrodynamic radii of DNA condensates were measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) on a Delsa Nano particle analyzer (Beckman Coulter). The conformational change of DNA on binding with Cu was determined by circular dichroism spectra on a J-1500 spectropolarimeter (Jasco, UK). Each sample was scanned thrice at the scan rate of 50nm/min and quartz cell having 1mm path length. The thermal melting of dsDNA before and after Cu NCs formation was studied by optical melting experiments using a Peltier controlled UV–vis spectrophotometer (Bioquest, Cecil, UK). The circular dichroism, thermal melting studies were performed by using 20μM of DNA (with and without Cu NCs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: ITC measurements
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the thermodynamic parameters of Cu NCs formation in dsDNA as a template, isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) studies were done at 25°C at pH 7 using MicroCal ITC200 (GE, USA). For titration, 2μL aliquots of CuSO4solution (40μL, 200μM) was titrated with DNA solution (280μL, 20μM) in MOPS buffer at pH 7with stirring speed of 700rpm for a total injection of 20. The titration was performed by keeping the initial molar ratio of DNA and Cu(II)=1:10 where DNA act as a donor and Cu(II) as an acceptor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Creation and analysis of abasic site in oligonucleotides
ce:section.ce:para: The uracil residues in the individual DNA strands C1ab, C2ab and oligomer duplex C1ab-C2ab (0.5 nmol) were treated with 10 units of UDG enzyme for 1h at 37°C in 1×UDG buffer [39]. The UDG treated sample was incubated with 10 units of APE1 enzyme at 37°C for 12h. The generation of abasic site in DNA was confirmed by running the sample through electrophoresis on 20% denaturing PAGE in 1X tris taurine EDTA (TTE) buffer. The oligo containing abasic site is indicated by C1ab and C2ab.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of DNA from onion and purification
ce:section.ce:para: The DNA from onion was extracted by blending one small onion for 2–3min following the method reported by Nordell et al. [40]. A carbonate buffer solution was prepared containing 0.75g of NaCl, 2.5g of NaHCO3, and 1mL of liquid detergent triton in 60mL of distilled water and kept at 4°C. Equal volume of the onion paste solution and the carbonate buffer solution were mixed with stirring and heated for 2min at 70°C. The mixture was centrifuged to collect the supernatant containing DNA. The collected filtrate was then added to 3M sodium acetate solution and mixed properly. The chilled ethanol solution was carefully added to the above solution to get DNA precipitate out from it. The onion DNA precipitate was purified by ethanol wash several times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of DNA from HeLa cells
ce:section.ce:para: The human cervical cancer cell lines HeLa, were grown to confluency in DMEM with 10% FBS and 100 units/mL of penicillin/streptomycin. The cells (1×104/mL) were seeded in 96 well flat bottom plates and the medium was removed followed by cell wash with PBS buffer. To these cells, 50μL of cell lysis buffer (10mM Tris-Cl, 10mM NaCl, 10mM EDTA, 0.5% w/v SDS) was added to each well. The microtiter plate was placed in polypropylene box and incubated for 12h at 60°C in an oven. The cells were allowed to cool and 150μL of NaCl/ethanol solution (5M NaCl in 10mL ethanol) was added to each well and kept for 30min at room temperature. The precipitate of DNA was obtained and washed with ethanol several times to purify.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Creation of abasic site in pUC19, plant (onion) DNA and DNA extracted from HeLa cells
ce:section.ce:para: Abasic sites in all samples of genomic DNA were formed by chemical treatment following the method reported by Luke et al. 5μg of DNA sample was incubated in buffer solution containing 10mM sodium citrate, 10mM NaCl and 10mM NaH2PO4 at pH5 for 1h at 70°C [41]. The citrate buffer treated DNA samples were then collected and purified by ethanol wash at 4°C several times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DNA condensate formation from linear pUC19
ce:section.ce:para: Linear plasmid was obtained by treatment of supercoiled form with HindIII restriction endonuclease enzyme. 5μg of pUC19 was incubated with 50 units of HindIII restriction endonuclease for 1h at 37°C in 1×HindIII restriction buffer. The linear plasmid was purified by ethanol precipitation. The DNA condensate formation was carried out in dust free condition and all the solutions were filtered through 0.2μm Millipore filters. The linear pUC19 was condensed in 10mM sodium phosphate buffer at pH 7 using polyamine spermidine. Equal volume of solution of 5μg of DNA and 200μM of spermidine were added to a total volume of 20μL [42]. The DNA condensate formation was analysed by measuring the hydrodynamic radii of solution using DLS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of N,S co-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal heating of citric acid and cysteamine as a carbon, nitrogen and sulfur source. In the synthesis, citric acid (10mmol, 2.10g) and cysteamine (10mmol, 0.73g) were dissolved in 15mL water and evaporated at 80°C for 8h. The viscous reaction mixture was transferred into a teflon-lined autoclave and further heated at 150°C for 6h. The crude solution was diluted and centrifuged at 12,000rpm and washed to remove the agglomerated particles and unreacted organic moieties. The light yellow solution containing CDs was filtered through 0.2μm filter for purification. The purified CDs were stored at 4°C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of Cu NCs and estimation by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The DNA templated Cu NCs were formed by adding 5μM of each of the DNA strands complementary to each other (C1 and C2 or their abasic site containing counterpart) in MOPS buffer (10mM MOPS, 150mM NaCl, 1mM MgCl2 at pH 7.5) [43]. The two ssDNA solution was annealed by heating at 90°C and cooled down to room temperature for DNA hybridization. To this dsDNA solution, 1mM of ascorbic acid was added in a final volume of 180μL and incubated for 10min at 37°C. Then, 20μL of 100μM CuSO4 was added to it and allowed to react for 15min at 37°C in dark. The process was repeated for other dsDNA C1abC2, C1C2ab and C1abC2ab templates. The fluorescent Cu NCs obtained through this process was stored at 4°C for further characterization.

To estimate the unreacted free Cu(II) ions in solution after formation of DNA templated Cu NCs, the quenching of CDs in the presence of free ions was evaluated. The solution of CDs (3mL, 5μg/mL) was incubated with Cu NCs created on all DNA templates and mixture of CuSO4/ascorbic acid as a control solution (concentration was kept same as used for Cu NCs formation) to estimate the quenching of CDs in the presence of free Cu(II) ions. The visual change in fluorescence intensity of CDs was observed with 1 lesion in 74 nucleotides, all the spectra shown for visual change of Cu NC’s and CD’s fluorescence is at 60nM of DNA. Further, interactions of each DNA duplex without Cu ions, on the fluorescence intensity of CDs were also checked as a control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Creation and analysis of abasic sites in DNA
ce:section.ce:para: Abasic sites in oligomer duplex C1ab-C2ab was created by UDG treatment of the DNA where the uridine bases at precise locations in C1ab and C2ab (Table 1) were removed. Successful generation of abasic sites in the ssDNA and DNA duplex C1ab-C2ab was confirmed by APE1 enzyme induced DNA cleavage at the abasic site through gel electrophoresis (Fig. 1 A). Abasic sites were generated in linearized plasmid and onion genomic DNA by treating the DNA samples with citrate buffer at pH 5, at 70°C. Abasic site generation in all DNA samples was followed by NaOH treatment and analysis on 1.5% agarose gel to ascertain the formation of abasic sites. From agarose gel image, it was observed that citrate buffer treated DNA samples show considerable fragmentation following NaOH treatment indicating the presence of abasic sites in them (Fig. 1B and C). After confirmation of abasic site formation in the DNA samples from gel electrophoresis, these DNAs were used as substrates for templated Cu NC formation in further downstream studies.

A portion of the linearized plasmid was also converted to DNA condensates with spermidine after abasic site generation in the former. The polycation spermidine neutralizes the negatively charged phosphate backbone of DNA to form a highly ordered and compact structure of DNA. The spermidine treated linear plasmid was analysed by DLS and the hydrodynamic diameter was found to be ∼120nm which confirms the formation of close compact DNA condensate formation (Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DNA abasic sites by Cu NCs and CDs
Synthesis and characterisation of Cu NCs in oligonucleotide duplex
ce:section.ce:para: Cu NCs were synthesized in C1-C2 oligomer duplex as a control. The Cu NCs formation was also studied in C1ab-C2 and C1-C2ab dsDNA as well as in C1ab-C2ab. The later represents a two component bistranded clustered abasic sites located at12th position from 5′ in C1 and at 25th position from 5′ in C2. Herein, the oligomer sequences are designed to possess cohesive end (5′-AGCT) which is compatible for HindIII restriction endonuclease enzyme digest for introducing more complexity in recombinant DNA after ligation and insertion for expanding future substrate scope.

The absorption and fluorescence spectra of Cu NCs on all the four oligomeric DNA templates were recorded as shown in Fig. 2 . The maximum absorption peak of Cu NCs was observed at ∼340nm and emission at ∼585nm. Evidently, in the presence of abasic sites in C1ab-C2, C1-C2ab and C1abC2ab there is a decrease in fluorescence intensity of the Cu NCs indicating their reduced formation in those DNA samples (Fig. 2B). However, the comparative decrease in fluorescence with increase in the number of abasic site as in C1ab-C2ab could not be visually differentiated under UV light following Cu NC fluorescence quenching (Fig. 3 C and D). Herein, the concept of introducing CD was wedged to trap the free Cu ions that are present in solution and seek a visual detection as described in the subsequent section.

The formation of Cu NC templated on C1-C2 was confirmed from TEM analysis (Fig. 2C) that reveals spherical morphology of the Cu NCs having size distribution from 1 to 3nm in diameter that are typical of such nanoparticles. From TEM analysis, the probable number of Cu atoms constituting one nanocluster was found to be ∼4 to 12. However, no indication was obtained regarding the difference in Cu NCs formation in the oligomeric DNA with abasic sites from TEM images (Fig. 2D). Thus, TEM analysis is to confirm only that DNA templated Cu NC formation takes place under the given experimental conditions.

The oxidation state of Cu in Cu NCs on DNA templates with (C1abC2ab) and without abasic sites (C1C2) were analysed by XPS. The XPS spectra (Fig. 2E) of Cu NCs (C1C2) shows two dominant peaks at 933.2eV and 951.5eV which can be assigned to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 electrons respectively for Cu in Cu(I)/Cu(0) oxidation state [44]. It has been observed that the difference in binding energies of Cu(0) and Cu(I) is 0.1eV which suggest the oxidation state of Cu in Cu NCs may be 0 or 1. On the other hand, the XPS spectra of Cu NCs with C1abC2ab DNA template shows additional peaks at 934.5eV, 942.7eV, 953.4eV and 959.2eV along with the peaks for Cu(I)/Cu(0). The prominent peak at 934.5eV and 953.4eV can be assigned to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 electrons respectively and peaks at 942.7eV and 959.2eV are the satellite peaks for Cu(II) [45,46]. Therefore, the Cu NCs on clustered abasic sites containing DNA templates indicate presence of Cu(II) which can be tracked with CDs.

Reportedly, nucleobases play a vital role for Cu NCs formation by reduction of Cu(II) to Cu(0) through intermediate Cu(I) [44,47]. In oligonucleotide duplexes thymines are replaced by uracil at precise locations in the DNA sequence that is subsequently removed by UDG treatment to form abasic sites in DNA. With the insertion of abasic site in DNA, there is less formation of Cu NCs and the template containing clustered abasic site proved to be the least favourable template for NCs formation due to decreased efficiency of Cu(II) reduction to Cu(0).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of CDs in presence of abasic sites in DNA
ce:section.ce:para: Reportedly, CDs have been used as a sensor for the detection of Cu(II) by charge transfer mechanism [48]. In our study, on addition of Cu(II) in ascorbic acid/DNA, the nucleobases in dsDNA template induces the reduction of Cu(II) to Cu(0)/Cu(I) to form NCs. However, DNA containing abasic sites are found to be inefficient template for NCs formation which results in availability of free Cu(II) in solution which binds to surface of CDs and results in fluorescence quenching of CDs [49]. In presence of multiple closely located abasic sites as in C1ab-C2ab, the quenching was considerably higher as compared to the oligomeric duplexes having single abasic sites. Interestingly, the comparative quenching of CDs was visually detectable when solutions are irradiated with simple hand held UV lamp (365nm) or transilluminator (Fig. 3E and F). This is important, since this phenomenon offer a methodology to detect the presence of complex closely located abasic sites in cluster that are potentially mutagenic without much machine intervention.

The water soluble CDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of precursors citric acid and cysteamine. The maximum absorption of CD was observed at 330nm and emission at 430nm at the excitation wavelength of 330nm (Fig. S2). The CD shows excitation wavelength dependent emission from 430 to ∼480nm. The estimation of copper in free ionic form in solution was evaluated by using CDs as a tracking agent. The CD emission was found to decrease as the concentration of free copper ions in solution increases (Fig. 3A, B). Thus, in the absence of appropriate binding site in DNA, the free Cu(II) in solution get adsorbed on the surface of the CDs. The charge transfer from CDs to the adsorbed Cu(II) takes place that subsequently decrease the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. Notably, the fluorescence quenching is proportionate to the number of Cu(II) getting adsorbed over the CD surface. Hence, the quenching directly relates to the number of abasic sites present in a DNA sample that otherwise act as a template for reduction of Cu(II) to Cu(0) nanoclusters.

Reportedly, quantum dots and CDs have shown remarkable interaction with DNA [50,51]. Herein, to confirm the quenching of CD’s fluorescence by free Cu ions exclusively, the interaction of DNA and CDs were examined by gel electrophoresis, UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra. The dsDNA was mixed with CDs solution and analysed on 1.5% agarose gel. The two separate bands from the mixture of CD+ dsDNA indicate no interaction or electrostatic repulsion between these negatively charged species (Fig. S3A). Further, from UV spectra and fluorescence spectra the influence of DNA on CD’s absorbance and fluorescence was found to be negligible (Fig. S3B and C) which validate the quenching of CD’s fluorescence due to free Cu ions in reaction mixture of Cu NCs. The feasibility and sensitivity of the present study were also compared with other recently developed abasic site detection methods (Table 2 ). The present method demonstrates an easy, economic and fast detection of abasic sites in DNA compared to other methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Change in conformational and duplex stability of DNA induced by Cu NCs formation
ce:section.ce:para: The formation of Cu NCs on the oligomer duplex template is accompanied by subtle conformational changes in the DNA. Circular dichroism spectra of C1-C2 illustrates a typical B-form of DNA with one positive band at 278nm and one negative band at 249nm. However, after formation of Cu NCs and their insertion in DNA’s grooves, notable changes in the intensity of both the positive and the negative peak was observed. This suggests that although the B form of the DNA is maintained, considerable conformational stress has been induced through formation of Cu NCs. Nevertheless, the decrease in the positive and negative band intensity with the formation of Cu NCs in C1ab-C2 and C1-C2ab was less pronounced. Interestingly, the difference in intensity of the positive and negative peaks nearly vanishes for NCs formation in C1ab-C2ab. The change in the intensity of DNA peak on copper incorporation in DNA duplex in the order- C1C2>C1abC2>C1C2ab>C1abC2ab (Fig. 4 ) is due to more prominent formation of Cu NCs in fully matched C1C2compared to C1abC2, C1C2ab and least formation on C1abC2ab template which is in agreement with UV-fluorescence data.

The formation of DNA templated Cu NC formation affects the thermal stability of the oligomer duplexes. The melting profile for all four DNA duplexes was measured and the strands containing abasic sites were found to show biphasic melting (Fig. S4) [52]. The two transition temperatures for abasic sites containing DNA may be attributed to asymmetric position of the lesion along DNA. The melting temperature (Tm) of C1-C2 was found to decrease by ∼2.7°C on NCs formation which indicates decrease in duplex stability of DNA through the binding of Cu (Fig. 5 ). The incorporated Cu NCs destabilize the base stacking and results in partial unwinding of the duplex. The trend of decrease in the Tm was found to be exactly similar to those observed in the circular dichroism analysis (Fig. S4, Table S1). While, the decrease in Tm for C1abC2and C1C2ab is ∼2.2°C and ∼1.8°C respectively, there is hardly any difference for C1ab-C2ab before and after Cu NC formation (Fig. 5) following the same trend as in circular dichroism analysis. Thus, Cu NCs formation leads to higher destabilization of fully matched C1C2 due to efficient binding while abasic site containing oligomer duplex DNA are little affected. In case of the presence of cluster abasic sites in DNA, where the duplex already has compromised conformational and thermal stability, the Cu NC formation in considerably hindered and thus unable to introduce further changes in the duplex parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding efficiency of Cu NCs to DNA
ce:section.ce:para: The binding event of Cu after cluster formation in oligomer duplex DNA was monitored through isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) [53]. CuSO4 solution was titrated against DNA samples that are preincubated with ascorbic acid, the reducing agent for Cu(II). The binding event of the in-situ formed Cu NC on DNA was fitted for single site of binding and the thermodynamic parameters were obtained. The negative peak in the graph indicates the heat released in an exothermic process when CuSO4 get reduced and strongly binds to dsDNA. With the increase in concentration of ligand i.e. Cu ions with time, the availability of binding sites in DNA decreases as indicated by smaller peaks showing saturation of binding sites. From ITC measurements, the binding of Cu to dsDNA and subsequent formation of Cu NCs was found to be a spontaneous process as indicated by negative change of enthalpy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of abasic sites in plasmid, plant genomic DNA and DNA from HeLa cells
Fluorescence quenching of CDs in presence of abasic sites in plasmid and plant genomic DNA
ce:section.ce:para: A sensitive biosensing platform should respond to the target present in various forms of the host. As a proof of principle, DNA templated Cu NCs formation and subsequent quenching of CD fluorescence in the presence of abasic sites were analysed in plasmid DNA pUC19 in linear and condensed form and onion genomic DNA.

The unbound Cu(II) on these DNA templates having abasic sites was examined by using CDs. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was found to decrease with increase in free copper ions concentration in abasic site containing linear pUC19 (Fig. 7 ). However, the intensity of CDs was not much affected in case of DNA condensate (data not shown). This is due to spermidine induced condensate formation that acts as a deterrent for Cu NC formation using the compact DNA as a template indicating that DNA to be tested for the presence of abasic sites need to be decondensed. However, the results for onion genomic DNA are encouraging that clearly show quenching of CD’s fluorescence after induction of abasic sites in the DNA sample. Most importantly, the change in CD fluorescence could be easily detected through visual inspection under UV light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of CDs in presence of abasic sites in real biological sample
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of suggested strategy, the abasic site detection was carried out in real biological sample using HeLa cells. The purified DNA from HeLa cells were used as a template for Cu NCs formation. Further, the abasic sites in DNA were created by using sodium citrate buffer at 70°C and used for Cu NCs formation. The two Cu NCs solution with and without abasic sites containing DNA templates were incubated with CDs solution. The visual difference in fluorescence intensities of CDs added to these Cu NCs solution makes it possible to differentiate normal DNA from abasic site containing one easily (Fig. 8 A&B). The results validate that the proposed strategy is an easy and suitable method for detection of abasic sites in DNA by using hand held UV light even in biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: In summary, detection of solitary and clustered abasic sites DNA damage using fluorescence properties of Cu NCs and CDs is demonstrated here. Oligomeric DNA duplex with single or clustered abasic sites was used as template for Cu NC formation. Reduced formation of Cu NCs in those samples was observed compared to the normal duplex that present more free Cu(II) ions in solution. The free copper ions in turn get adsorbed over CDs in solution and quantitatively quench the CD’s fluorescence. The quenching of CD fluorescence was visually detectable and is proportional to the number of abasic sites present in the DNA. The hindered formation of Cu NCs in abasic site containing DNA was also confirmed through circular dichroism and DNA melting studies. While, fully matched normal DNA duplex shows appreciable conformational and thermal instability, abasic site containing DNA was found to be less responsive to the Cu NC formation. Thermodynamic parameters of the binding of Cu with DNA also validate the results obtained from spectroscopic measurements. The binding process of Cu(II) to DNA was found to be enthalpically driven process and binding constant is highest for fully matched DNA without any abasic site and is least for template containing clustered abasic sites. The concept of abasic site detection in DNA has been demonstrated to work in linear plasmid, onion genomic DNA and DNA from HeLa cells, whereby quenching of CD fluorescence was detected visually. However, unavailability of open form of DNA as host imparts barrier to incorporation and binding of Cu(II) and subsequent NCs formation in condensed form of DNA. Nevertheless, this method is able to detect single abasic site in ∼35 base pair long DNA. Under our experimental conditions, nanomolar range of DNA solution is sufficient to offer a visual observation of fluorescence quenching of CDs. In an obvious extrapolation, extensive damage in DNA in a sample could be detected in a few minutes with the help of just a handheld UV lamp. Most importantly, potentially mutagenic clustered abasic sites could be easily detectable owing to the remarkable difference in CD fluorescence intensity compared to normal or even single abasic site containing DNA in an oligomer duplex. Evidently, this method offers a fast, economic and reliable biosensing platform for abasic site DNA damage lesions that could be researched further for detection of other types of DNA damage lesions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Author contributions
ce:section.ce:para: The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant to P. D. and M. K. Singh from BRNS, Department of Atomic Energy (DAE), Govt. of India, Project No.2012/34/59/BRNS. S. S. is thankful to BRNS and IIT Patna for fellowship. The authors are thankful to IIT Patna and BARC (Mumbai) for infrastructure and other facilities, SAIF and Central Surface Analytical Facility of IIT Bombay for TEM and XPS facilities. Gift of HeLa cell by Prof. K. K. Sinha, NIPER Hajipur, India is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.08.100.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para: Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI) available: Dynamic light scattering intensity distribution of DNA condensate created from linearized pUC19, UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of CDs and CD+DNA solution, agarose gel electrophoresis showing repulsive interaction of CDs and DNA, thermal melting graph showing change in thermal melting temperature of dsDNA before and after Cu NCs formation, isothermal titration calorimetry graph for Cu NCs formation in DNA templates, table S1 and table S2.
The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Seema Singh received her MSc (Chemistry) degree from RTM Nagpur University, India. She is a PhD student in department of chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology, Patna. Her research area is nanoparticle-DNA system for biomedical application.

Manoj K. Singh is a Scientist ‘F’, Atomic & Molecular Physics Division in Bhabha Atomic Research Center (BARC), Mumbai. He is a Postdoctoral fellow from University of Kansas, USA. He has research and development experience of spectroscopic techniques at BARC.

Prolay Das is an Associate Professor in IIT Patna, India. He is mainly engaged in DNA nanotechnology based research. His PhD is from Georgia Tech and Postdoctoral fellow from Brookhaven National Laboratory, USA.
